title: Credential Compromise via Mismatched Identity Fragment Combinations
narrative: Fraudster possesses valid card credentials and legitimate identity fragments (correct name, valid email, verified billing address) but the specific COMBINATION of these elements has never been seen together in the cardholder's transaction history. Each data point passes individual verification checks, but the pairing of addr1 with this P_emaildomain, or this card with this M_id, represents a first-time occurrence. Elevated C-field counters reveal the card is being used across an abnormally diverse set of identity contexts. The fraudster is mixing-and-matching stolen identity fragments to construct plausible-looking transactions that bypass basic fraud rules. Device fingerprinting shows the DeviceInfo string has no history with this card-identity combination. Transaction amounts remain within the cardholder's normal spending range to avoid amount-based triggers. This pattern exploits the gap between validating individual identity elements versus validating their historical co-occurrence patterns.
indicators: D1 = 0 indicating first-time pairing of DeviceInfo fingerprint with this card-identity combination
C13 > 24 (p90), card seen across unusually high count of distinct addr1-P_emaildomain pairs in rolling window
card1_distinct_addr1 > 6 and card1_distinct_products > 8, elevated diversity metrics for shipping contexts
C8 > 29 (p90) while C14 < 3 (below p75), high card-email diversity but low card-address-product stability
addr1-P_emaildomain combination not found in cardholder's transaction history (first-time pairing flag)
TransactionAmt between 75-180 (p25-p75 range), amount deliberately kept within cardholder's typical range
V315 > 0.72 (p90 threshold), Vesta engineered feature flagging identity-fragment mismatch anomaly
indicator_explanations: The device fingerprint has never been used with this specific card and identity pairing before
Card is appearing with far more distinct combinations of shipping addresses and email domains than normal
Card shows elevated diversity in both shipping destinations and product categories purchased
Card appears across many different email addresses but with limited consistent shipping-address patterns
This billing address and email domain have never been used together for this card previously
Purchase amount fits the cardholder's normal spending profile to avoid threshold-based detection
Advanced model feature detects unusual pattern in how identity elements are combining across transactions
summary: Valid credentials are being used with legitimate identity fragments that individually pass verification, but the specific combinations of address, email domain, and card have never appeared together in the cardholder's history. Elevated card-counter diversity metrics reveal the card is being paired with an abnormally wide range of identity contexts.
reasoning: Legitimate cardholders exhibit stable patterns in how their identity elements combine across transactions. When valid credentials appear with verified-but-never-before-paired identity fragments while card-counter diversity metrics spike, it indicates a fraudster is mixing stolen identity data to construct plausible transactions that bypass element-level validation.
